content.text: The Strangler Fig pattern, originally described for legacy system modernisation, is particularly well-suited to AI feature migrations. When replacing a rule-based system with an LLM-powered agent, or upgrading an existing AI feature to a new model or architecture, a direct cutover is high risk because AI behavior is probabilistic and not fully expressible in unit tests. The strangler pattern mitigates this by running the new system alongside the old in shadow mode, gradually increasing traffic to the new system as evaluation evidence accumulates, and retaining the ability to revert instantly at any phase.

The Strangler Fig pattern in the context of AI feature migration is a deployment strategy where the new AI system gradually 'strangles' the old system by capturing increasing fractions of production traffic while the old system remains fully operational as a fallback. The migration proceeds in controlled phases, each gated by evaluation evidence that the new system matches or exceeds the old system's behavior on the dimensions that matter.

For agentic AI migrations, the pattern has three phases: shadow mode (the new AI system receives all requests but its outputs are discarded; only logged for comparison), canary phase (the new system serves a small, increasing percentage of real traffic while the old system handles the rest), and full migration (the new system handles 100% of traffic; the old system is kept in standby for emergency rollback). Each phase transition is controlled by a feature flag and requires a formal evaluation gate.

Direct cutover of an AI feature is dangerous because AI behavior is non-deterministic and not fully captured by any evaluation suite. A new LLM model might perform better on 95% of inputs while catastrophically failing on a specific subcategory. A shadow mode deployment catches these tail failures in production data before real users are affected. The ability to ramp traffic incrementally—1% → 5% → 25% → 100%—allows teams to observe behavior at each step and halt the migration if problems emerge.

The strangler pattern is also valuable for AI feature migrations because it decouples the development of the new system from its deployment timeline. The ML team can iterate on the new model in shadow mode for weeks, observing divergences from the legacy system, improving the new model's behavior, and building confidence before any real user sees the new output.
Risk-controlled migration: each phase is gated by evaluation evidence, preventing a bad migration from reaching full production.
Instant rollback at any phase: the feature flag can redirect all traffic back to the legacy system in seconds, without a code deployment.
Shadow comparison data: running both systems simultaneously produces a gold dataset of cases where they disagree, which is invaluable for targeted improvement.
Gradual confidence building: teams accumulate empirical evidence of the new system's behavior across the full production input distribution, not just the evaluation test suite.
No deadline pressure: the old system continues serving users normally throughout the migration, removing urgency from the transition.
Behavioral baseline: the legacy system's production behavior serves as the ground truth for comparison during shadow mode.
Double infrastructure cost: during shadow or canary phases, both systems run simultaneously, doubling compute and API call costs.
Comparison complexity: determining whether the new system's divergences from the old are improvements or regressions requires evaluation expertise.
Feature flag overhead: the traffic splitting logic adds complexity to the request routing layer.
Migration length: a thorough strangler migration can take weeks or months; teams impatient for the new system may push to skip phases.
Legacy system maintenance: the old system must remain functional and monitored throughout the migration, requiring ongoing maintenance effort.

The implementation requires three components: a shadow/canary router that sends requests to both systems based on a configurable traffic percentage, a comparison logger that records the inputs and outputs of both systems when they diverge, and an evaluation gate runner that checks whether the new system meets the parity threshold before each phase transition.

The comparison logger is the key analytical tool. Every request where the old and new systems produce different outputs is stored as a divergence record. These records are reviewed by the ML team during the shadow phase to classify each divergence as either an improvement (the new system is better) or a regression (the old system is better). Once the regression rate is below the threshold, the team proceeds to the canary phase.

attrs.alt: Strangler pattern migration phases from shadow mode through canary to full AI traffic with safety gates

content.text: The architecture has a centralized traffic router that consults a feature flag to determine the traffic split. In shadow mode (split = 0%), all requests go to the legacy system but are also asynchronously forwarded to the new system; its responses are logged but never served to users. In canary mode (split = 5-50%), a percentage of requests are served by the new system; if it errors or times out, the request automatically falls back to the legacy system. In full migration (split = 100%), all traffic goes to the new system; the legacy system stands by for emergency kill-switch activation.
Replacing a rule-based or ML classifier with an LLM-based classifier where exact output parity is not expected.
Upgrading a major model version (e.g., from claude-3-5-sonnet to claude-3-7-sonnet) where behavioral differences are expected.
Migrating an agentic workflow from one architecture to another (single-agent to multi-agent, sequential to parallel tool execution).
Moving from a hosted prompt service to an inline prompt, or vice versa, where the exact prompt assembly may differ.
Any migration where a behavioral regression on a subset of production inputs would be costly to discover post-full-cutover.

# eval_gate.py - Evaluation gate for strangler migration phase transitions
from pathlib import Path
import json
import sys

PHASE_THRESHOLDS = {
    "shadow_to_canary": {
        "regression_rate": 0.05,   # <5% of shadow divergences are regressions
        "improvement_rate": 0.10,  # >10% are improvements
    },
    "canary_to_full": {
        "regression_rate": 0.02,   # <2% regressions at canary traffic level
        "error_rate_new": 0.001,   # new system error rate <0.1%
        "latency_p99_ratio": 2.0,  # new system p99 latency < 2x legacy
    },
}

def load_divergences(log_path: str) -> list[dict]:
    return [json.loads(l) for l in Path(log_path).read_text().splitlines() if l]

def classify_divergences(divergences: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    Classify each divergence as improvement, regression, or neutral.
    In practice this uses LLM-as-judge; here simplified to example.
    """
    improvements, regressions, neutral = 0, 0, 0
    for d in divergences:
        # Placeholder: real classification uses eval framework
        label = d.get("classification", "neutral")
        if label == "improvement":
            improvements += 1
        elif label == "regression":
            regressions += 1
        else:
            neutral += 1
    total = len(divergences)
    return {
        "total": total,
        "improvement_rate": improvements / total if total else 0,
        "regression_rate": regressions / total if total else 0,
    }

def check_phase_gate(transition: str, divergences: list[dict]) -> bool:
    """Check if the migration can advance to the next phase."""
    thresholds = PHASE_THRESHOLDS[transition]
    stats = classify_divergences(divergences)
    print(f"Phase gate check for: {transition}")
    print(f"  Total divergences: {stats['total']}")
    print(f"  Regression rate: {stats['regression_rate']:.1%} (threshold: {thresholds['regression_rate']:.1%})")
    print(f"  Improvement rate: {stats['improvement_rate']:.1%}")

    if stats["regression_rate"] > thresholds["regression_rate"]:
        print("GATE FAILED: regression rate too high")
        return False
    print("GATE PASSED: safe to advance migration phase")
    return True

if __name__ == "__main__":
    transition = sys.argv[1]
    log_path = sys.argv[2]
    divs = load_divergences(log_path)
    passed = check_phase_gate(transition, divs)
    sys.exit(0 if passed else 1)
Migration of a keyword-based intent classifier to an LLM-based intent classifier:

new LLM classifier runs in shadow on 100% of requests. 2,000 divergences logged. Classification: 1,400 improvements (LLM correctly handles ambiguous queries), 120 regressions (LLM over-routes edge-case queries to wrong intent), 480 neutral.
prompt updated to fix the 120 regression cases. Re-run shadow comparison: 1,600 improvements, 30 regressions. Gate check passes (regression rate 1.5% < 5% threshold).
LLM classifier serves 5% of real traffic. User satisfaction scores unchanged. Error rate 0.08% (well under 0.1% threshold). Latency p99 1.4x legacy (under 2x threshold).
incremental ramp, each step cleared by gate check. All metrics stable.
100% traffic to LLM classifier. Legacy system in standby. Kill switch tested (0.3s to redirect all traffic back).

A travel booking company replaced its rule-based query parser (which classified travel intent using regex patterns) with an LLM-based parser. Direct cutover was ruled out because the rule-based parser had accumulated 3 years of edge-case fixes and the ML team needed production data to validate parity.

The strangler migration ran for 6 weeks. During shadow mode, 8% of divergences were classified as regressions. The team discovered that the LLM parser was mis-handling date expressions in unusual formats (e.g., 'the Thursday after next'). They added few-shot examples to the prompt to address these cases, reducing the regression rate to 1.8%. After a 10% canary week with no user complaints, they ramped to 100% over the following fortnight.
going straight to canary without shadow data means the first regression evidence comes from real users.
logging divergences without classifying them as improvements or regressions makes the shadow data useless for gate decisions.
jumping from 5% to 100% in one step misses the intermediate data that catches regressions on specific traffic subcategories.
Removing the legacy system too soon:
the legacy system must remain operable for at least 30 days after full migration as an emergency fallback.
the kill switch must be tested at full scale before the migration reaches 100%, not assumed to work.
Always start with shadow mode before any canary traffic; production data distribution is the best evaluation dataset.
Classify every divergence as improvement, regression, or neutral—do not just count divergences.
Define phase transition thresholds before the migration begins, not as you go.
Test the kill switch (instant rollback to legacy) before advancing past 25% canary.
Keep the legacy system fully operational and monitored for at least 30 days after full migration.
Use a feature flag to control the traffic split so rollback requires a config change, not a code deploy.
Document the migration plan, thresholds, and phase transition decisions in a migration log alongside the commit history.

Shadow mode doubles API call volume (every request calls both systems). Budget for this cost before starting. At high traffic, shadow calls can be sampled (e.g., 20% of requests rather than 100%) while still producing sufficient divergence data. The comparison logger must be async and fire-and-forget; blocking the main request path to complete the shadow call would double user-facing latency.

The strangler pattern improves security by ensuring the new AI system's behavior is thoroughly validated before it handles sensitive user data at full scale. During shadow mode, even if the new system produces incorrect outputs, users are unaffected. The kill switch—which must be tested and documented—provides a reliable reliability guarantee: if the new system causes issues after full migration, the team can revert all traffic in under a minute without a code deploy.
Use the strangler pattern to migrate AI features by running the new system in shadow mode before serving any real user traffic.
Shadow mode produces a divergence dataset that reveals regression cases that no pre-migration evaluation suite could anticipate.
Gate each phase transition with a formal evaluation check: shadow-to-canary requires regression rate below threshold; canary-to-full requires error rate and latency within bounds.
Control the traffic split with a feature flag so rollback is instant and requires no code deployment.
Keep the legacy system operational for 30 days after full migration as an emergency fallback.
Test the kill switch before reaching 100% canary; an untested rollback mechanism is not a reliable safety net.
